articles.title: Natural Language Processing and Graph Theory: Making Sense of Imaging Records in a Novel Representation Frame
articles.authors: Gonçalves LB; Nesic I; Obradovic M; Stieltjes B; Weikert T; Bremerich J
articles.keywords: NLP; graph theory; radiology; imaging records; temporal referrals; date extraction; health care
articles.abstract: Background: A concise visualization framework of related reports would increase readability and improve patient management. Temporal referrals to prior comparative exams are an essential connection in written radiology reports. NLP permits extraction of structured information from unstructured texts. Objective: This study proposes and evaluates an NLP-based algorithm for extracting temporal referrals in written radiology reports, applies it to 10 years of reports, and introduces a graphical representation of imaging reports. Methods: A CNN extracted referral dates from reports (precision, recall, F1 measured on 149 reports). Results: The model reliably extracted temporal referrals. The graph-theoretic representation improved readability and clinical utility. Conclusion: Combining NLP with graph theory provides a novel framework for structuring and visualizing patient imaging histories.

articles.title: An Accurate and Efficient Approach to Knowledge Extraction from Scientific Publications Using Structured Ontology Models, GNNs, and LLMs
articles.authors: Ivanisenko TV; Demenkov PS; Ivanisenko VA
articles.keywords: text-mining; GNN; LLM; knowledge graph; biomedical; NLP; ontology; ANDSystem
articles.abstract: The rapid growth of biomedical literature challenges researchers to stay current. PubMed alone contains over 37 million abstracts. This paper presents a hybrid GNN-LLM method to extract knowledge from scientific publications using structured ontology models. Applied to insomnia, the approach identified 25 interactions between 32 human proteins absent from known knowledge bases, including regulatory interactions between MAOA and 5-HT2C and a circadian regulatory loop involving RORB and NR1D1. The method can accelerate therapeutic target discovery by focusing expert verification on automatically extracted, high-relevance information.
